title: The Church, People of God
html:
Big Question

What is the Church? A building, an institution, or something more?

It was the morning of Pentecost. The disciples were hiding in the upper room, with the doors securely locked «for fear of the Jews» (Jn 20:19). Just fifty days earlier they had watched their Master crucified. Then risen, yes — but they were still afraid. They were Galilean fishermen with no education, no influence, no money, no plan. The project seemed dead before it was born.

But that morning everything changed. «Suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the whole house where they were sitting. And divided tongues as of fire appeared to them and rested on each one of them. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit» (Acts 2:2-4).

Those frightened men went out into the street transformed. Peter — the same one who had denied Christ three times — stood up before the crowd and preached with such power that three thousand people were baptized that day. The Church was born. Not as a carefully planned human organization, but as an explosion of fire and wind: the Holy Spirit taking possession of a handful of fragile men and turning them into the living Body of Christ in the world.

Years later, another man — Saul of Tarsus — was on the road to Damascus, «still breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord» (Acts 9:1). But when the midday sun exploded in a light brighter than the sun, when he fell to the ground and heard a voice saying, «Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?», his entire world collapsed. He was not persecuting a sect. He was persecuting a Person. And that Person identified himself with those he was chaining. «I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting» (Acts 9:5). The persecutor became a member of the Body he was persecuting. Thus was born Paul, who would later write: «Now you are the Body of Christ, and each one is a member» (1 Cor 12:27).

The Church was born at Pentecost with fire and wind. It grew through martyrs and confessors. It has reached us across twenty centuries of history. What is the Church to you?

The Church in God's Plan

The word «Church» comes from the Greek ekklesía, meaning «assembly called together.» It is not a building; it is a people gathered by God. The Catechism teaches that the Church is, above all, the people God has been gathering from the very beginning of the world (CCC 751-752). From Adam and Eve, through Noah, Abraham, Moses, and the prophets, God has been forming a people that belongs to him. But the Church is not a human invention or a political institution: it springs from the heart of God, who wants to gather all his scattered children (CCC 759-760). In its fullness, the Church was prepared by the Old Covenant, founded by Christ, and manifested by the Holy Spirit at Pentecost (CCC 758-768).

🌱 Activity

Draw a large tree. In the roots write «Abraham,» on the trunk «Moses» and «David,» on the branches «The Apostles» and «The first Christians,» and on the leaves write your name. Do you see how the Church started long before you were born? You are part of that same story. The tree would have no leaves without its roots.

The Catechism uses various images to speak about the Church (CCC 753-757): it is the sheepfold where Christ is the shepherd, the vineyard where he is the vine and we are the branches, the house of God built with living stones, the bride of Christ, the mother who begets children for God, and above all, the Body of Christ and the Temple of the Holy Spirit. No single image suffices on its own; together they help us understand the richness of the mystery of the Church. As Paul said, it is a «mystery hidden for ages and generations but now revealed to his saints» (Col 1:26).

The Church, People of God

The Second Vatican Council recovered a central image from Scripture: the Church as «People of God» (CCC 781-782). One does not enter this people by birth, race, or merit, but through faith and Baptism. All the baptized belong to this people with equal dignity. Peter expresses it beautifully: «But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession, that you may proclaim the excellencies of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy» (1 Pet 2:9-10).

The People of God has unique characteristics: it is a priestly people (all the baptized share in Christ's priesthood, offering their lives as spiritual worship), a prophetic people (called to proclaim the Gospel through their lives and words), and a royal people (called to reign by serving others, as Christ came to serve, not to be served) (CCC 783-786).

The People of God is not an elite or an exclusive club. It is a universal family, open to all peoples and cultures, because God desires all people to be saved (CCC 781). Its law is the new commandment of love (Jn 13:34), its mission is to be salt of the earth and light of the world, and its destiny is the Kingdom of God, already inaugurated on earth but made perfect in heaven (CCC 782).

🌿 Connection

Read 1 Pet 2:9-10. What does Peter say about the identity of Christians? What does it mean that we are a «royal priesthood»? How can you live out your baptismal priesthood in your home, your school, or your workplace? The baptismal priesthood means that everything you do — working, studying, caring for your family, serving — can be an offering to God.

The Church, Body of Christ and Temple of the Spirit

Paul uses a powerful image: the Church is the Body of Christ (CCC 787-789). Christ is the head and we are the members. But it is not an empty metaphor; it is real. Through Baptism we are incorporated into Christ, and through the Eucharist we are united to him intimately. «Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread» (1 Cor 10:17). And Paul goes further: «Christ loved the Church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the Church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish» (Eph 5:25-27).

In this body there is diversity of members with different gifts and functions, but all are necessary. «If the whole body were an eye, where would the sense of hearing be?» (1 Cor 12:17). No member is more important than another; what matters is unity in Christ. «If one member suffers, all suffer together; if one member is honored, all rejoice together» (1 Cor 12:26). This unity in diversity is one of the most beautiful realities of the Church: each one has a different gift, and all gifts are necessary (CCC 790-795).

🪴 Reflection

The image of the Body of Christ tells us that we cannot be Christians alone. Have you tried to live your faith in isolation? What do you miss when you don't have community? What gifts can you bring to the body? Think about your concrete talents: can you listen? sing? teach? cook for others? visit the sick? All of this builds up the Church.

Furthermore, the Church is the Temple of the Holy Spirit (CCC 797-798). The Holy Spirit is the soul of the Church, the one who gives it life, who guides it, who distributes its gifts. Like the soul in the human body, the Holy Spirit is present in the entire Body of Christ: he is the principle of its life, its unity, and its fruitfulness. On the day of Pentecost, the Spirit descended upon the Apostles and the Church was born for mission. It is the Spirit who makes the Church the universal sacrament of salvation, the sign and instrument of communion with God and of the unity of the entire human race (CCC 775-776, 799-801). The Spirit distributes charisms — special gifts — to build up the Church, and also grants special graces to the faithful of every condition (CCC 798-801).

One, Holy, Catholic, and Apostolic

The Creed confesses that the Church is «one, holy, catholic, and apostolic.» These are not qualities the Church has given itself, but gifts it receives from Christ and that define it. They are the four marks or «notes» of the true Church (CCC 811-812).

One: The Church is one because it is the Body of one Christ. It has one Lord, one faith, one Baptism (Eph 4:4-6). Christ himself prayed to the Father «that they may all be one» (Jn 17:21). Unity is both a gift and a task. Although there is a painful division among Christians, the Catholic Church believes that full unity subsists in her, and she works tirelessly for the unity of all Christians through ecumenism (CCC 813-822). The bonds of unity are: confession of one faith, common celebration of worship, and apostolic succession (CCC 815).

Holy: The Church is holy because its founder is Christ, the Holy One of God, and the Holy Spirit dwells within it. Not because its members are perfect — the Church is made up of sinners — but because Christ continually sanctifies it and makes it a source of holiness through the sacraments. It is at once «chaste and sinful»: holy in its Head, in need of purification in its members (CCC 823-829). The Church canonizes its saints precisely to show that holiness is possible and that Christ's work shines in her. In every saint, the Church recognizes her own calling to holiness.

Catholic: Catholic means «universal.» The Church is not for one group, one culture, or one era; it is for everyone. Christ sent his Apostles «to the whole world» (Mk 16:15) and told them «go therefore and make disciples of all nations» (Mt 28:19). The Church is present throughout the world, and in each local community — every diocese, every parish — the whole Church is present with all its means of salvation (CCC 830-835). It is not a federation of local Churches; it is the one Church of Christ truly present in each of them (CCC 833-835).

Apostolic: The Church was built on the foundation of the Apostles (Eph 2:20). Christ chose twelve Apostles, gave them his authority, and sent them to preach the Gospel. That mission continues today through apostolic succession: the bishops, in communion with the Pope — successor of Peter — are the legitimate successors of the Apostles (CCC 857-865). Apostolicity means that the Church preserves and faithfully transmits the Apostles' teaching through Tradition and Sacred Scripture, interpreted by the living Magisterium (CCC 857).

🌳 Going Deeper

The four marks of the Church (one, holy, catholic, and apostolic) are like the coordinates of its identity. Read Eph 4:4-6 and Mt 16:18-19. How does Christ's promise to Peter («You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my Church») relate to the unity and apostolicity of the Church? Peter is the visible foundation of unity: he is the sign and servant of the communion of all the faithful in the one Church.

The Hierarchy: Pope, Bishops, and Priests

Christ did not leave his Church without shepherds. He chose twelve Apostles, and among them Peter, to whom he said: «You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my Church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it. I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven, and whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven» (Mt 16:18-19). Jesus himself instituted a structure of governance and service in his Church (CCC 874-877).

The Pope — Successor of Saint Peter and Bishop of Rome — is the perpetual and visible source and foundation of the Church's unity. As supreme pastor, he has full authority over the whole Church. But this authority is not worldly power: it is service. The Pope is the «Servant of the Servants of God.» When he teaches solemnly on faith and morals «ex cathedra» (from the chair of Peter), he enjoys the charism of infallibility, a gift of the Holy Spirit to preserve the Church from error (CCC 880-891).

Bishops are the successors of the Apostles. Each bishop receives the fullness of the Sacrament of Holy Orders and governs a particular Church (diocese) in communion with the episcopal college and under the authority of the Pope (CCC 886, 894-896). When bishops teach in union with the Pope, they too enjoy the charism of infallibility in matters of faith and morals (CCC 891).

Priests (presbyters) are collaborators with the bishops. They receive the Sacrament of Holy Orders in its second degree, and their mission is to preach the Gospel, celebrate the sacraments — especially the Eucharist — and shepherd the faithful in their parishes or communities (CCC 1562-1568, 1590-1591). They are not «religious functionaries»; they are shepherds configured to Christ the Head and Shepherd.

Deacons receive Holy Orders in the degree of service: they assist bishops and priests, proclaim the Gospel, baptize, assist at marriages, preside at funerals, and dedicate themselves to works of charity (CCC 1569-1571).

The Laity and the Sensus Fidelium

The Church is not just hierarchy. The vast majority of its members are lay people: Christian faithful who, incorporated into Christ by Baptism, live their vocation in the midst of the world's realities — family, work, culture, politics, science. Their specific mission is «to seek the Kingdom of God by engaging in temporal affairs and ordering them according to God's plan» (CCC 898-899). The laity are not «second-class Christians»; they are full members of the People of God, called to holiness and the apostolate (CCC 898-913).

The vocation of the laity is irreplaceable. They are the ones who bring the Gospel to places only they can reach: the office, the workshop, the hospital, the university, Congress. The Second Vatican Council emphasized that the laity have the right and duty to participate actively in the life of the Church according to their gifts and state in life (CCC 899, 907-908).

Another precious gift is the sensus fidelium (sense of the faith): the whole body of the faithful, anointed by the Holy Spirit in Baptism, cannot err in matters of belief. This «supernatural sense of the faith» is manifested when the faithful — from the bishops down to the last layperson — show a universal consent in matters of faith and morals (CCC 92-93, 785, 904). The sensus fidelium is not an opinion poll; it is the voice of the Holy Spirit resonating in the heart of the People of God, always in harmony with the Magisterium.

Mary, Mother of the Church

The Virgin Mary holds a unique place in the Church. She is, at once, a member of the Church — the first redeemed by Christ in a singular way (the Immaculate Conception) — and Mother of the Church, because she is Mother of Christ, its Head. At Calvary, Jesus said to John: «Behold, your mother!» (Jn 19:27). In John, all the disciples were represented. Mary is Mother of every member of the Body of Christ (CCC 963-970).

Mary is the model of the Church: in her faith («Blessed is she who believed»), in her charity (at the foot of the cross), and in her perfect union with Christ (CCC 967). She is also a powerful intercessor: from heaven, Mary continues to obtain for us the gifts of eternal salvation through her manifold intercession. She is not a goddess nor a rival of Christ; she is the first disciple, the one who best listened to and kept the Word, the one who said «Do whatever he tells you» (Jn 2:5) (CCC 968-969).

Saint Paul VI solemnly proclaimed Mary as «Mother of the Church» at the closing of the third session of the Second Vatican Council in 1964. The Church has always venerated the Virgin with a special worship — «hyperdulia» — which, while being absolutely inferior to the worship of adoration due only to God («latria»), is superior to the worship due to the other saints («dulia») (CCC 971).

Mission: Evangelization to All Nations

The Church does not exist for itself. It exists to evangelize: to proclaim the Good News of Jesus Christ to all peoples to the ends of the earth. Before ascending to heaven, Christ gave his Apostles the missionary mandate: «Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age» (Mt 28:18-20).

Evangelization is the essential mission of the Church. As the Second Vatican Council states, «the pilgrim Church is missionary by her very nature» (CCC 850). This mission is carried out in many ways: through the explicit preaching of the Gospel (mission ad gentes), through the witness of life of every Christian, through charity that transforms social structures, and through prayer and suffering offered for the world's salvation (CCC 849-856).

Every baptized person is a missionary by the very fact of being a member of the Church. You don't need to go to another continent. The first mission is right where you are: in your family, your workplace, your circle of friends. Being a missionary means living in such a way that your life makes the Gospel you proclaim with your lips believable. And it also means praying and contributing materially so that the Gospel reaches those who do not yet know it (CCC 900, 904-907).

Biblical Connection: «You are Peter»

And I tell you, you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my Church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it. I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven, and whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven. Matthew 16:18-19

This passage is the founding charter of the Church. At Caesarea Philippi, beside the immense rock where pagans worshiped the god Pan, Jesus builds his Church upon another rock: Simon, now Peter. He changes his name — just as God changed Abram's name to Abraham — because he is creating something new. He entrusts him with the keys, a symbol of authority in the royal household. Peter and his successors will be the visible sign of the unity Christ desires for his Church. It is not Peter's Church; it is Christ's Church built upon Peter. As the Catechism says: «The Lord Jesus endowed his community with a structure that will remain until the Kingdom is fully achieved» (CCC 765, 880-881).

For Reflection

  1. When you think of the Church, what is the first thing that comes to mind: an institution, a building, a community? Where does that image come from? After this lesson, has anything changed?
  2. Pentecost shows that the Church was born of the Holy Spirit, not human effort. Do you trust that the Spirit continues to guide the Church today, despite all difficulties? How can you open yourself more to his action?
  3. Paul says the Church is the Body of Christ and that we are all members of one another. Have you experienced faith in community, or do you tend to live it alone? What does the Christian community give you, and what can you give to it?
  4. The Church is holy but made up of sinners. Do you find it hard to see the Church as holy when you see the errors and sins of its members, even its pastors? How do you reconcile those two realities? Does it help to know that the saints also loved the Church with its wounds?
  5. Do you value the guidance of the Pope and bishops in your life of faith, or do you see them as something distant? How can you pray more for them and feel more united to the universal Church?

Key Takeaway

Key Takeaway: The Church is not a building you go to; you are it, together with all the baptized, the living Body of Christ in the world. Born from the pierced side of Christ on the cross, made manifest at Pentecost with fire and wind, the Church is your family, your mission, and your home.

Cultural Resources

Bernini's Baldacchino in Saint Peter's (1624-1633) — In the heart of Saint Peter's Basilica in Rome stands this imposing bronze canopy, a masterpiece of the Baroque. It stands 29 meters tall — as tall as a ten-story building — directly over the tomb of Saint Peter. Bernini designed it with four twisted Solomonic columns that seem to ascend toward heaven, crowned by angels and globes. The entire baldacchino proclaims: here is Peter, and upon this rock Christ built his Church. The bronze, partly taken from the Pantheon, symbolizes that paganism has been conquered by the Christian faith.

The Coptic Martyrs of Libya (2015) — On February 15, 2015, the Islamic State executed 21 Coptic Christians on a beach in Libya. They were humble Egyptian laborers. In the video of their execution, several of them murmur «Lord, have mercy» as they are beheaded. They did not deny Christ. The Coptic Church immediately recognized them as martyrs and inscribed them in the Synaxarium (book of saints). Pope Francis said: «They were killed for the sole fact of being Christians... The blood of our Christian brothers is a witness that cries out.» Their martyrdom reminds us that the Church is still persecuted and that the seed of martyrs remains the blood of new Christians.

Saint John XXIII and the Second Vatican Council — Angelo Roncalli was elected Pope in 1958. Many thought he would be a transitional Pope. But in 1959 he convoked the Second Vatican Council (1962-1965), which profoundly renewed the Church's life. He is said to have remarked: «Let's let in a little fresh air.» The Council recovered the image of the Church as People of God, emphasized the role of the laity, promoted ecumenism and dialogue with the world, and renewed the liturgy. It was a new Pentecost of the 20th century. John XXIII was canonized in 2014.

The Mexican Cristeros (1926-1929) — During the religious persecution in Mexico, thousands of Catholics — farmers, young people, priests — rose up in defense of their faith. Under the cry of «¡Viva Cristo Rey!» («Long live Christ the King!»), they faced the federal army. Many died as martyrs, among them the young Saint José Sánchez del Río, age 14, who was tortured and executed for refusing to renounce his faith. The Cristeros are a Mexican witness of how the laity lived their belonging to the Church to the ultimate consequences. Saint José Sánchez del Río was canonized by Pope Francis in 2016.

Saint Juan Diego and the Tilma of Guadalupe — In December 1531, the Virgin Mary appeared to a humble indigenous man, Juan Diego, on Tepeyac Hill. She asked him to go to the bishop and request the building of a temple. As proof, the Virgin caused roses to bloom in the middle of winter and left her image imprinted on Juan Diego's tilma. That tilma, made of maguey fiber that normally disintegrates within decades, remains intact after nearly 500 years. The image of Guadalupe united indigenous people and Spaniards in one faith and is today the heart of the Mexican Church and patroness of all the Americas. Juan Diego, canonized in 2002, is a model of the layperson who listens to Mary and carries out his mission — «Go to the bishop» — with humility and trust.
